chapter: Chapter 4: Linear Equations in Two Variables

questions.question: Define a linear equation in two variables and give two examples.
questions.answer: A linear equation in two variables is an equation of the form ax + by + c = 0 where a, b and c are constants and a and b are not both zero. Examples include x + y = 5 and 2x + 3y = 7.

questions.question: Explain the concept of a solution of a linear equation in two variables with an example.
questions.answer: A solution of a linear equation in two variables is a pair of values of x and y that satisfies the equation. For example, in the equation x + y = 5, the pair (2,3) is a solution because 2 + 3 = 5.

questions.question: Explain how ordered pairs represent solutions of linear equations.
questions.answer: Solutions of linear equations are written as ordered pairs (x, y). Each ordered pair represents values of x and y that satisfy the equation. When substituted into the equation, both sides become equal.

questions.question: Explain why a linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions.
questions.answer: A linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions because many different pairs of x and y values can satisfy the equation. Each pair forms a point on the straight-line graph.

questions.question: Explain how to check whether a given ordered pair is a solution of a linear equation.
questions.answer: To check whether a pair (x,y) is a solution, substitute the values of x and y into the equation. If the left side equals the right side, the pair is a solution.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of coefficients in a linear equation.
questions.answer: In a linear equation ax + by + c = 0, the numbers a and b are called coefficients of x and y. They indicate how the variables are multiplied in the equation.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of constant term in a linear equation.
questions.answer: In the equation ax + by + c = 0, the term c is called the constant term because it does not contain any variable.

questions.question: Explain how substitution helps to find solutions of linear equations.
questions.answer: Substitution involves replacing the variables with specific values and checking whether the equation becomes true.

questions.question: Explain how the equation x + y = 5 can have many solutions.
questions.answer: Different pairs like (1,4), (2,3), (3,2) and (4,1) satisfy x + y = 5. Therefore it has many solutions.

questions.question: Explain how to convert an equation into standard form.
questions.answer: To convert an equation into standard form, move all terms to one side so that the equation becomes ax + by + c = 0.

questions.question: Explain the graph of a linear equation in two variables.
questions.answer: The graph of a linear equation in two variables is a straight line. Every point on the line represents a solution of the equation.

questions.question: Explain how to find solutions of x + y = 6.
questions.answer: Choose values of x and find corresponding y values so that x + y = 6. Example: (2,4), (3,3), (4,2).

questions.question: Explain the role of variables in linear equations.
questions.answer: Variables represent unknown quantities that can take different values in the equation.

questions.question: Explain how to verify if (4,1) is a solution of 3x + y = 13.
questions.answer: Substitute x=4 and y=1. 3(4)+1=13 so the pair is a solution.

questions.question: Explain the importance of linear equations in mathematics.
questions.answer: Linear equations help represent relationships between two quantities and are widely used in algebra, geometry and real-life problems.

questions.question: Explain how x = 2 can be written as a linear equation in two variables.
questions.answer: x = 2 can be written as x + 0y = 2.

questions.question: Explain how y = 3 can be written as a linear equation in two variables.
questions.answer: y = 3 can be written as 0x + y = 3.

questions.question: Explain how solutions of linear equations can be listed.
questions.answer: Choose different values of x and calculate corresponding y values to form ordered pairs.

questions.question: Explain the concept of straight line graphs in linear equations.
questions.answer: Linear equations form straight line graphs because the variables change at a constant rate.

questions.question: Explain how the equation x + y = 8 represents many ordered pairs.
questions.answer: Pairs such as (2,6), (3,5), (4,4) all satisfy the equation.

questions.question: Explain how to identify coefficients in 5x + 3y − 4 = 0.
questions.answer: Coefficient of x is 5, coefficient of y is 3, and constant term is −4.

questions.question: Explain why x² + y = 5 is not a linear equation.
questions.answer: Because the variable x has power 2. Linear equations must have variables of degree 1.

questions.question: Explain how the equation x + y = 10 can have infinite solutions.
questions.answer: Many ordered pairs like (1,9), (2,8), (3,7) satisfy the equation.

questions.question: Explain how substitution helps verify solutions.
questions.answer: Substitution replaces variables with given values and checks whether both sides become equal.

questions.question: Explain how linear equations represent relationships between two quantities.
questions.answer: Linear equations show how one variable changes when the other changes and represent this relationship using algebraic expressions.
